doom.driver: Ebola death toll reaches 600, as new cases suspected in other parts of Congo.
doom.blurb: EBOLA FINDS NEW ZIP CODES IN CONGO
doom.summary: Six hundred dead and the outbreak is now bleeding into previously unaffected regions of Congo — the classic sign that containment is losing. Meanwhile the U.S.-Iran ceasefire is somehow still technically alive via 'technical talks,' which is diplomatic for 'we're arguing about who shot first.' With a 1,000-km typhoon bearing down on Taiwan, wildfires killing a dozen in Spain, and Ebola going on tour, the planet is workshopping several apocalypse scripts simultaneously.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: EU Parliament greenlit Chat Control 1.0, introducing mass surveillance of private messages across Europe — a significant digital rights rollback.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: U.S.-Iran 'technical talks' continue after consecutive days of strikes, with the ceasefire in legal limbo and oil demand set for its first annual decline since 2020.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: GPT-5.6 receives government greenlight and rolls out broadly alongside ChatGPT Work, accelerating autonomous agentic AI deployment in professional settings.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Ebola death toll hits 600 with suspected new cases in previously unaffected Congo regions, signaling active geographic spread beyond the Ituri epicenter.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: Anthropic's glimpse inside Claude's 'hidden conceptual space' is fascinating science, but reveals how little we still understand about what's actually happening inside frontier models.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — night scene aftermath, all light from artificial sources, deep navy and smoldering orange palette, camera positioned at ground level inside the outer perimeter of the Evangelical Medical Center in Bunia, looking across a red laterite courtyard still puddled with disinfectant and rainwater, orange sodium lamp mounted on a concrete post casting a single hard cone of amber light that reflects broken in the wet ground, a row of empty white Tyvek coveralls hanging limp and deflated on a wire strung between two corrugated-iron-roofed outbuildings, rubber gumboots lined in a row beneath them like abandoned skins, a backpack chlorine sprayer lying on its side in the foreground with its wand nozzle trailing in a puddle, deep navy shadows consuming the building walls beyond the lamplight, a single window in the far institutional block glowing faint amber-yellow from within suggesting one worker still at their station, the dense equatorial vegetation beyond the fence pressing in from all sides as a wall of deep blue-black foliage, mud-tracked footprints crossing the courtyard toward a closed metal door with its paint flaking — the whole scene reading as exhausted, still, and quietly devastated, the aftermath of a night shift that never really ends.

dawn.driver: China lands reusable rocket for first time, state media says. It follows similar landings of reusable rockets by US-owned companies SpaceX and Blue Origin.
dawn.blurb: HUMANITY'S REUSABLE ROCKET CLUB JUST GOT BIGGER
dawn.summary: Against a backdrop of military escalation and geopolitical friction, China has achieved its first successful reusable rocket landing — joining SpaceX and Blue Origin in a club that barely existed a decade ago. Whatever one thinks of the rivalries driving it, the spread of reusable launch capability means humanity now has multiple independent pathways to space, reducing fragility and expanding the long-term odds of our becoming a multi-planetary species. Competition in rockets has historically moved the technology faster than cooperation alone ever did, and today's milestone is proof that the dream keeps compounding.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Reusable rocket technology reaching a third major actor dramatically lowers the cost curve for all future space access.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: China's engineers openly acknowledge following the trail blazed by SpaceX and Blue Origin, a rare implicit nod to shared human achievement.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Humanoid robots performing their first live surgical procedures on pigs at UC San Diego signals that science is still relentlessly pushing forward even amid global crisis.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: World oil demand is forecast to post its first annual decline since 2020, a quiet but structural signal of the energy transition taking hold.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Spider venom has been found to kill varroa mites — the parasite decimating global honeybee populations — without harming the bees themselves, a potentially huge win for food security.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Ground-level view looking straight up from below, camera pressed flat against a cold concrete floor, gazing up at the underside of a massive flatscreen television mounted high on a break room wall, the screen glowing with a distant rocket descent feed, warm amber and teal light washing down from above. A single aerospace engineer stands directly beneath it, neck craned back, face tilted fully upward toward the camera lens, expression cracked open in disbelief and joy, fists clenched at her sides, a paper coffee cup forgotten and tilting in one hand, a thin stream of coffee beginning to fall toward the camera in a perfect arc. The break room around her is spartan — a metal folding chair, a wall calendar, a half-eaten meal on a table edge — all rendered in deep amber side-light with long dramatic shadows stretching toward the viewer. The television's glow forms a second warm rectangle of light on the ceiling above her, teal-blue from the ocean feed contrasting the golden ambient warmth of the room. Deep golden hour amber and warm teal, radiant, triumphant, the enormity of the moment carried entirely in one upturned human face.
